root_text.an6.63:1.2: Taṁ suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.an6.63:1.3: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.an6.63:2.2: Kāmā, bhikkhave, veditabbā, kāmānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, kāmānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, kāmānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, kāmanirodho veditabbo, kāmanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:3.1: Vedanā, bhikkhave, veditabbā, vedanānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, vedanānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, vedanānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, vedanānirodho veditabbo, vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:4.1: Saññā, bhikkhave, veditabbā, saññānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, saññānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, saññānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, saññānirodho veditabbo, saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:5.1: Āsavā, bhikkhave, veditabbā, āsavānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, āsavānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, āsavānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, āsavanirodho veditabbo, āsavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:6.1: Kammaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ, kammānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, kammānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, kammānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, kammanirodho veditabbo, kammanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:7.1: Dukkhaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ, dukkhassa nidānasambhavo veditabbo, dukkhassa vemattatā veditabbā, dukkhassa vipāko veditabbo, dukkhanirodho veditabbo, dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā.
root_text.an6.63:8.1: ‘Kāmā, bhikkhave, veditabbā, kāmānaṁ nidānasambhavo veditabbo, kāmānaṁ vemattatā veditabbā, kāmānaṁ vipāko veditabbo, kāmanirodho veditabbo, kāmanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbā’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:8.4: cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā,
root_text.an6.63:8.8: kāyaviññeyyā phoṭṭhabbā iṭṭhā kantā manāpā piyarūpā kāmūpasaṁhitā rajanīyā.
root_text.an6.63:8.9: Api ca kho, bhikkhave, nete kāmā kāmaguṇā nāmete ariyassa vinaye vuccanti—
root_text.an6.63:11.2: Añño, bhikkhave, kāmo rūpesu, añño kāmo saddesu, añño kāmo gandhesu, añño kāmo rasesu, añño kāmo phoṭṭhabbesu.
root_text.an6.63:12.2: Yaṁ kho, bhikkhave, kāmayamāno tajjaṁ tajjaṁ attabhāvaṁ abhinibbatteti puññabhāgiyaṁ vā apuññabhāgiyaṁ vā, ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, kāmānaṁ vipāko.
root_text.an6.63:13.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo kāmanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:13.4: sammādiṭṭhi, sammāsaṅkappo, sammāvācā, sammākammanto, sammāājīvo, sammāvāyāmo, sammāsati, sammāsamādhi.
root_text.an6.63:14.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ kāme pajānāti, evaṁ kāmānaṁ nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kāmānaṁ vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kāmānaṁ vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kāmanirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kāmanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, so imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti kāmanirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:14.2: Kāmā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… kāmanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:14.3: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:15.1: Vedanā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:15.2: iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ. Kiñcetaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ?
root_text.an6.63:15.4: sukhā vedanā, dukkhā vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā.
root_text.an6.63:17.2: Atthi, bhikkhave, sāmisā sukhā vedanā, atthi nirāmisā sukhā vedanā, atthi sāmisā dukkhā vedanā, atthi nirāmisā dukkhā vedanā, atthi sāmisā adukkhamasukhā vedanā, atthi nirāmisā adukkhamasukhā vedanā.
root_text.an6.63:18.2: Yaṁ kho, bhikkhave, vediyamāno tajjaṁ tajjaṁ attabhāvaṁ abhinibbatteti puññabhāgiyaṁ vā apuññabhāgiyaṁ vā,
root_text.an6.63:19.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:20.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ vedanaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ vedanānaṁ nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ vedanānaṁ vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ vedanānaṁ vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ vedanānirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ vedanānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti. So imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti vedanānirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:20.2: Vedanā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:20.3: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:21.1: Saññā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:21.2: iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ. Kiñcetaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ?
root_text.an6.63:21.4: rūpasaññā, saddasaññā, gandhasaññā, rasasaññā, phoṭṭhabbasaññā, dhammasaññā.
root_text.an6.63:23.2: Aññā, bhikkhave, saññā rūpesu, aññā saññā saddesu, aññā saññā gandhesu, aññā saññā rasesu, aññā saññā phoṭṭhabbesu, aññā saññā dhammesu.
root_text.an6.63:24.3: Yathā yathā naṁ sañjānāti tathā tathā voharati, evaṁ saññī ahosinti.
root_text.an6.63:25.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:26.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ saññaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saññānaṁ nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saññānaṁ vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saññānaṁ vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saññānirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ saññānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, so imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti saññānirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:26.2: Saññā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti.
root_text.an6.63:26.3: Iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:27.1: Āsavā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… āsavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:27.2: iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ. Kiñcetaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ?
root_text.an6.63:29.2: Atthi, bhikkhave, āsavā nirayagamanīyā, atthi āsavā tiracchānayonigamanīyā, atthi āsavā pettivisayagamanīyā, atthi āsavā manussalokagamanīyā, atthi āsavā devalokagamanīyā.
root_text.an6.63:30.2: Yaṁ kho, bhikkhave, avijjāgato tajjaṁ tajjaṁ attabhāvaṁ abhinibbatteti puññabhāgiyaṁ vā apuññabhāgiyaṁ vā, ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, āsavānaṁ vipāko.
root_text.an6.63:31.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo āsavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:32.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ āsave pajānāti, evaṁ āsavānaṁ nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ āsavānaṁ vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ āsavānaṁ vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ āsavānaṁ nirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ āsavānaṁ nirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, so imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti āsavanirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:32.2: Āsavā, bhikkhave, veditabbā …pe… āsavanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti.
root_text.an6.63:32.3: Iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:33.1: Kammaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ …pe… kammanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:35.2: Atthi, bhikkhave, kammaṁ nirayavedanīyaṁ, atthi kammaṁ tiracchānayonivedanīyaṁ, atthi kammaṁ pettivisayavedanīyaṁ, atthi kammaṁ manussalokavedanīyaṁ, atthi kammaṁ devalokavedanīyaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:36.3: diṭṭheva dhamme, upapajje vā, apare vā pariyāye.
root_text.an6.63:37.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo kammanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:38.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ kammaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kammānaṁ nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kammānaṁ vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kammānaṁ vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kammanirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ kammanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, so imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti kammanirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:38.2: Kammaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ …pe… kammanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti,
root_text.an6.63:38.3: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:39.1: Dukkhaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ, dukkhassa nidānasambhavo veditabbo, dukkhassa vemattatā veditabbā, dukkhassa vipāko veditabbo, dukkhanirodho veditabbo, dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti.
root_text.an6.63:39.2: Iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ, kiñcetaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ?
root_text.an6.63:39.3: Jātipi dukkhā, jarāpi dukkhā, byādhipi dukkho, maraṇampi dukkhaṁ, sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsāpi dukkhā, yampicchaṁ na labhati tampi dukkhaṁ, saṅkhittena pañcupādānakkhandhā dukkhā.
root_text.an6.63:41.2: Atthi, bhikkhave, dukkhaṁ adhimattaṁ, atthi parittaṁ, atthi dandhavirāgi, atthi khippavirāgi.
root_text.an6.63:42.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco yena dukkhena abhibhūto pariyādinnacitto socati kilamati paridevati, urattāḷiṁ kandati, sammohaṁ āpajjati, yena vā pana dukkhena abhibhūto pariyādinnacitto bahiddhā pariyeṭṭhiṁ āpajjati:
root_text.an6.63:42.3: ‘ko ekapadaṁ dvipadaṁ jānāti imassa dukkhassa nirodhāyā’ti?
root_text.an6.63:42.4: Sammohavepakkaṁ vāhaṁ, bhikkhave, dukkhaṁ vadāmi pariyeṭṭhivepakkaṁ vā.
root_text.an6.63:43.3: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo dukkhassa nirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.an6.63:44.1: Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako evaṁ dukkhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ dukkhassa nidānasambhavaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ dukkhassa vemattataṁ pajānāti, evaṁ dukkhassa vipākaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ dukkhanirodhaṁ pajānāti, evaṁ dukkhanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti, so imaṁ nibbedhikaṁ brahmacariyaṁ pajānāti dukkhanirodhaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:44.2: Dukkhaṁ, bhikkhave, veditabbaṁ, dukkhassa nidānasambhavo veditabbo, dukkhassa vemattatā veditabbā, dukkhassa vipāko veditabbo, dukkhanirodho veditabbo, dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā veditabbāti.
root_text.an6.63:44.3: Iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an6.63:45.1: Ayaṁ kho so, bhikkhave, nibbedhikapariyāyo dhammapariyāyo”ti.

translation_text.an6.63:1.1: “Mendicants, I will teach you a penetrative exposition of the teaching.
translation_text.an6.63:1.2: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.an6.63:2.1: “Mendicants, what is the penetrative exposition of the teaching?
translation_text.an6.63:2.2: Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:3.1: Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:4.1: Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:5.1: Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:6.1: Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:7.1: Suffering should be known. And its source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known.
translation_text.an6.63:8.1: ‘Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ That’s what I said,
translation_text.an6.63:8.2: but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:8.3: There are these five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.an6.63:8.4: Sights known by the eye, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.an6.63:8.8: Touches known by the body, which are likable, desirable, agreeable, pleasant, sensual, and arousing.
translation_text.an6.63:8.9: However, these are not sensual pleasures. In the training of the Noble One they’re called ‘kinds of sensual stimulation’.
translation_text.an6.63:8.10: Greedy intention is a person’s sensual pleasure.
translation_text.an6.63:8.11: The world’s pretty things aren’t sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an6.63:8.12: Greedy intention is a person’s sensual pleasure.
translation_text.an6.63:8.13: The world’s pretty things stay just as they are,
translation_text.an6.63:8.14: but the attentive remove desire for them.
translation_text.an6.63:9.1: And what is the source of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.an6.63:11.1: And what is the diversity of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.an6.63:11.2: The sensual desire for sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and touches are all different.
translation_text.an6.63:11.3: This is called the diversity of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an6.63:12.1: And what is the result of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.an6.63:12.2: When one who desires sensual pleasures generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds—this is called the result of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an6.63:13.1: And what is the cessation of sensual pleasures?
translation_text.an6.63:13.2: When contact ceases, sensual pleasures cease.
translation_text.an6.63:13.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of sensual pleasures is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:13.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:14.1: When a noble disciple understands sensual pleasures in this way—and understands their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation—they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of sensual pleasures.
translation_text.an6.63:14.2: ‘Sensual pleasures should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:14.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:15.1: ‘Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:15.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:16.1: And what is the source of feelings?
translation_text.an6.63:17.1: And what is the diversity of feelings?
translation_text.an6.63:17.2: There are pleasant feelings of the flesh, pleasant feelings not of the flesh, painful feelings of the flesh, painful feelings not of the flesh, neutral feelings of the flesh, and neutral feelings not of the flesh.
translation_text.an6.63:17.3: This is called the disparity of feelings.
translation_text.an6.63:18.1: And what is the result of feelings?
translation_text.an6.63:18.2: When one who feels generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds—
translation_text.an6.63:18.3: this is called the result of feelings.
translation_text.an6.63:19.1: And what is the cessation of feelings?
translation_text.an6.63:19.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:19.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:20.1: When a noble disciple understands feelings in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of feelings.
translation_text.an6.63:20.2: ‘Feelings should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:20.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:21.1: ‘Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:21.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:21.4: perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas.
translation_text.an6.63:22.1: And what is the source of perceptions?
translation_text.an6.63:23.1: And what is the diversity of perceptions?
translation_text.an6.63:23.2: The perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas are all different.
translation_text.an6.63:23.3: This is called the diversity of perceptions.
translation_text.an6.63:24.1: And what is the result of perceptions?
translation_text.an6.63:24.2: Communication is the result of perception, I say.
translation_text.an6.63:24.3: You communicate something in whatever manner you perceive it, saying ‘That’s what I perceived.’
translation_text.an6.63:24.4: This is called the result of perceptions.
translation_text.an6.63:25.1: And what is the cessation of perception?
translation_text.an6.63:25.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:25.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:26.1: When a noble disciple understands perception in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of perception.
translation_text.an6.63:26.2: ‘Perceptions should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:26.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:27.1: ‘Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:27.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:27.4: the defilements of sensuality, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
translation_text.an6.63:28.1: And what is the source of defilements?
translation_text.an6.63:28.2: Ignorance is the source of defilements.
translation_text.an6.63:29.1: And what is the disparity of defilements?
translation_text.an6.63:29.2: There are defilements that lead to rebirth in hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, the human world, and the world of the gods.
translation_text.an6.63:29.3: This is called the disparity of defilements.
translation_text.an6.63:30.1: And what is the result of defilements?
translation_text.an6.63:30.2: When one who is ignorant generates a corresponding incarnation, partaking of either good or bad deeds—this is called the result of defilements.
translation_text.an6.63:31.1: And what is the cessation of defilements?
translation_text.an6.63:31.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of defilements is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:31.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:32.1: When a noble disciple understands defilements in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of defilements.
translation_text.an6.63:32.2: ‘Defilements should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:32.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:33.1: ‘Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’ That’s what I said,
translation_text.an6.63:33.2: but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:33.3: It is intention that I call deeds.
translation_text.an6.63:33.4: For after making a choice one acts
translation_text.an6.63:33.5: by way of body, speech, and mind.
translation_text.an6.63:34.1: And what is the source of deeds?
translation_text.an6.63:35.1: And what is the disparity of deeds?
translation_text.an6.63:35.2: There are deeds that lead to rebirth in hell, the animal realm, the ghost realm, the human world, and the world of the gods.
translation_text.an6.63:35.3: This is called the disparity of deeds.
translation_text.an6.63:36.1: And what is the result of deeds?
translation_text.an6.63:36.2: The result of deeds is threefold, I say:
translation_text.an6.63:36.3: in this very life, on rebirth in the next life, or at some later time.
translation_text.an6.63:36.4: This is called the result of deeds.
translation_text.an6.63:37.1: And what is the cessation of deeds?
translation_text.an6.63:37.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of deeds is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:37.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:38.1: When a noble disciple understands deeds in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of deeds.
translation_text.an6.63:38.2: ‘Deeds should be known. And their source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to their cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:38.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:39.1: ‘Suffering should be known. And its source, disparity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:39.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an6.63:39.3: Rebirth is suffering; old age is suffering; illness is suffering; death is suffering; sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are suffering; not getting what you wish for is suffering. In brief, the five grasping aggregates are suffering.
translation_text.an6.63:40.1: And what is the source of suffering?
translation_text.an6.63:40.2: Craving is the source of suffering.
translation_text.an6.63:41.1: And what is the disparity of suffering?
translation_text.an6.63:41.2: There is suffering that is severe, mild, slow to fade, and quick to fade.
translation_text.an6.63:41.3: This is called the disparity of suffering.
translation_text.an6.63:42.1: And what is the result of suffering?
translation_text.an6.63:42.2: It’s when someone who is overcome and overwhelmed by suffering sorrows and wails and laments, beating their breast and falling into confusion. Or else, overcome by that suffering, they begin an external search, wondering:
translation_text.an6.63:42.3: ‘Who knows one or two phrases to stop this suffering?’
translation_text.an6.63:42.4: The result of suffering is either confusion or a search, I say.
translation_text.an6.63:42.5: This is called the result of suffering.
translation_text.an6.63:43.1: And what is the cessation of suffering?
translation_text.an6.63:43.3: The practice that leads to the cessation of suffering is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.an6.63:43.4: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.an6.63:44.1: When a noble disciple understands suffering in this way … they understand that this penetrative spiritual life is the cessation of suffering.
translation_text.an6.63:44.2: ‘Suffering should be known. And its source, diversity, result, cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation should be known.’
translation_text.an6.63:44.3: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an6.63:45.1: This is the penetrative exposition of the teaching.”

variant_text.an6.63:2.2: kāmanirodho → kāmānaṁ nirodho (mr) | kāmanirodhagāminī → kāmanirodhagāmini (pts1ed); kāmānaṁ nirodhagāminī (mr)
variant_text.an6.63:8.9: ca kho, bhikkhave, nete kāmā kāmaguṇā nāmete → te kāmaguṇā nāma nete kāmā (mr)
variant_text.an6.63:23.2: bhikkhave, saññā rūpesu, aññā saññā saddesu → aññā bhikkhave rūpesu saññā aññā saddesu saññā (mr)
variant_text.an6.63:24.2: Vohāravepakkaṁ → vohāravepakkāhaṁ (bj, sya-all, pts1ed) | saññaṁ → saññā (sya-all, pts1ed)
variant_text.an6.63:39.3: byādhipi dukkho → vyādhipi dukkho (bj); byādhipi dukkhā (sya-all, mr); vyādhipi dukkhā (pts1ed) | pañcupādānakkhandhā → pañcupādānakkhandhāpi (mr)
variant_text.an6.63:42.3: ko → so na (mr) | jānāti → pajānāti (bj, sya-all, mr)
